1. Using Keating

Keating is an open-source teaching tool and learning workspace. You may use the website, browser app, CLI, and desktop app for lawful learning, research, and educational work. You are responsible for the prompts, files, API keys, provider credentials, and outputs you use with Keating.

2. Accounts, keys, and providers

Keating can connect to third-party AI providers and local runtimes that you configure. Those providers may have their own terms, pricing, data-handling rules, and rate limits. Keep your API keys and credentials secure. Do not share keys through prompts, public sessions, screenshots, or exported artifacts.

3. Educational content, not professional advice

Keating is designed to support study and reasoning. It does not provide legal, medical, financial, or other professional advice. Verify important information with appropriate sources and qualified professionals before acting on it.

4. User content and shared sessions

You retain responsibility for the content you enter, upload, import, export, or share. If you create a share link or publish an artifact, make sure you have the right to share that material and that it does not include private information you did not mean to disclose.

5. Acceptable use

Do not use Keating to harm others, break the law, bypass security controls, infringe rights, distribute malware, or abuse third-party services. We may limit or disable hosted features if they are used in a way that threatens reliability, safety, or legal compliance.

6. Open-source software

Keating is distributed under the license terms in its public repository. Repository code, package releases, and documentation may include separate notices for open-source dependencies. If there is a conflict between this website notice and the repository license for source code, the repository license governs the source code.

7. Availability and changes

Keating is provided as-is and may change over time. Hosted pages, demos, downloads, experiments, and provider integrations may be updated, paused, or removed. We will try to keep important learning paths available, but we do not guarantee uninterrupted service.

8. Contact

For questions about these terms, open an issue in the Keating repository or use the contact channel listed on the project site.

Privacy Policy

Last updated: {LAST_UPDATED}

1. What Keating stores locally

Keating is designed around local-first learning data. Browser sessions, settings, provider preferences, and study artifacts may be stored in your browser or desktop app storage so the workspace can work without a central account. Clearing browser or app data may remove that local history.

2. Provider keys and AI requests

When you configure external AI providers, your prompts, attachments, and generated context may be sent to those providers to produce responses. Provider keys should stay in your local settings or in server-side environment variables for hosted routes. Do not paste secrets into chat messages or shared sessions.

3. Shared sessions and public links

If you create or open a shared session link, the shared content may be encoded in the URL or stored through the sharing mechanism used by the app. Anyone with access to a public share link may be able to read the shared conversation or artifact. Review the content before sharing.

4. Analytics and diagnostics

Keating may use analytics or diagnostic tooling on hosted web pages to understand product usage, reliability, and release health. We aim to collect product-level signals rather than sensitive learning content. Browser privacy settings, blockers, and consent controls may limit this collection.

5. Desktop and P2P storage

The desktop app is intended to keep learning data on your device and, when enabled, replicate data through peer-to-peer components. P2P topics and local writer identities are technical synchronization details, not a substitute for careful handling of private study material.

6. Third-party services

Keating can link to GitHub, AI providers, payment or credit systems, model hosts, and other services. Those services are governed by their own privacy policies. Review their terms before sending sensitive material to them.

7. Your choices

You can delete local browser data, remove saved provider keys, avoid creating share links, use local models where available, or stop using hosted features. For repository or hosted-site privacy questions, open an issue or use the contact channel listed on the project site.

import { Nav } from "../components/Nav"; import { Footer } from "../components/Footer"; import { DownloadPdfButton } from "../components/DownloadPdfButton"; import { useSeo } from "../hooks/useSeo"; const LAST_UPDATED = "July 1, 2026"; export function Terms() { useSeo({ title: "Terms of Service | Keating", description: "Terms of Service for Keating, the open-source hyperteacher and learning workspace at keating.help.", canonical: "https://keating.help/terms", }); return (
cat TERMS_OF_SERVICE.txt

Terms of Service

Last updated: {LAST_UPDATED}

1. Agreement to these terms

These Terms of Service (the “Terms”) govern your access to and use of Keating — the website at keating.help, the browser app, the command-line interface, and the desktop app (together, the “Service”). By using any part of the Service you agree to these Terms. If you do not agree, do not use the Service. If you use the Service on behalf of an organization, you represent that you have authority to bind that organization, and “you” includes it.

2. What Keating is

Keating is an open-source teaching tool and learning workspace. It is local-first: most of your data lives in your own browser storage or on your own device, and the Service connects to AI providers, runtimes, and sync mechanisms that you configure. Parts of the Service are hosted (the website, shared-session publishing, downloads); parts run entirely on your hardware.

3. Eligibility

You must be old enough to form a binding contract in your jurisdiction, or use the Service under the supervision of a parent, guardian, or educational institution that accepts these Terms on your behalf. The hosted Service is not directed at children under 13 (or the higher minimum age your jurisdiction requires for services like this one).

4. Your account-free workspace, keys, and providers

Keating does not require a central account. Instead, you may configure third-party AI providers, local runtimes, proxies, and peer-to-peer sync. Those third parties have their own terms, pricing, data-handling rules, and rate limits, and your use of them through Keating is governed by those agreements, not these Terms.

You are responsible for the API keys and credentials you enter. Keep them secure: do not paste keys into chat messages, shared sessions, exported artifacts, screenshots, or public issue reports. You are responsible for charges a provider bills against your keys, including charges resulting from requests Keating sends at your direction.

5. Your content

You retain all rights to the prompts, files, notes, study artifacts, quiz answers, and other material you create with or bring into Keating (“User Content”). We do not claim ownership of User Content. You are responsible for having the rights to the material you use, and for ensuring it does not violate law or the rights of others.

When you use a hosted feature that transmits User Content — for example publishing a shared session — you grant us the limited license needed to store, transmit, and display that content for the purpose of operating the feature, and to anyone you share the link with, until the content is removed.

6. Shared sessions and public links

Share links are effectively public: anyone who obtains a link may be able to read the shared conversation or artifact, and copies may be cached by browsers, intermediaries, or recipients even after removal. Review content before sharing, and do not share material containing personal data, credentials, or anything you lack the right to publish. We may remove shared content that violates these Terms or applicable law.

7. Acceptable use

You agree not to use the Service to:

  • violate any law or regulation, or infringe intellectual-property, privacy, or other rights;
  • harm, harass, defraud, or deceive others, or generate content intended to do so;
  • develop, distribute, or deploy malware, or bypass security or access controls of any system;
  • probe, disrupt, or overload the hosted Service or third-party services reached through it, including evading provider rate limits or terms;
  • misrepresent AI-generated output as human-authored where that matters (e.g., academic submissions governed by an integrity policy);
  • scrape, resell, or white-label the hosted Service in a way that suggests it is your own offering.

We may limit, suspend, or disable hosted features — including removing shared content — where use threatens reliability, safety, other users, or legal compliance.

8. Educational content, not professional advice

Keating supports study and reasoning. Output produced with Keating — including output from AI providers — may be inaccurate, incomplete, or outdated, and does not constitute legal, medical, financial, tax, or other professional advice. Verify important information with appropriate sources and qualified professionals before acting on it.

9. Open-source software and licenses

Keating’s source code is distributed under the license terms published in its public repository, which also carries notices for open-source dependencies. These Terms govern the hosted Service; the repository license governs the source code. If the two conflict with respect to source code, the repository license controls. Nothing in these Terms restricts rights granted to you by that license.

10. Fees

The hosted Service is currently provided without charge. Third-party providers you configure may charge you directly. If we introduce paid features, we will present their terms and pricing before you use them.

11. Privacy

Our Privacy Policy explains what data the Service stores locally, what is transmitted to providers and hosted components, and the choices you have. It is part of these Terms.

12. Disclaimer of warranties

THE SERVICE IS PROVIDED “AS IS” AND “AS AVAILABLE”, WITHOUT WARRANTIES OF ANY KIND, EXPRESS OR IMPLIED, INCLUDING WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY, FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE, NON-INFRINGEMENT, ACCURACY, OR UNINTERRUPTED AVAILABILITY. LOCAL-FIRST DATA LIVES IN YOUR BROWSER OR ON YOUR DEVICE; CLEARING BROWSER DATA, UNINSTALLING, OR DEVICE FAILURE MAY ERASE IT. KEEP YOUR OWN BACKUPS OF MATERIAL YOU CANNOT AFFORD TO LOSE.

13. Limitation of liability

TO THE MAXIMUM EXTENT PERMITTED BY LAW, THE KEATING MAINTAINERS AND CONTRIBUTORS WILL NOT BE LIABLE FOR ANY INDIRECT, INCIDENTAL, SPECIAL, CONSEQUENTIAL, OR EXEMPLARY DAMAGES — INCLUDING LOST DATA, LOST PROFITS, OR PROVIDER CHARGES — ARISING FROM OR RELATED TO YOUR USE OF THE SERVICE. TO THE SAME EXTENT, AGGREGATE LIABILITY FOR ALL CLAIMS RELATING TO THE SERVICE IS LIMITED TO THE GREATER OF THE AMOUNT YOU PAID US FOR THE SERVICE IN THE TWELVE MONTHS BEFORE THE CLAIM AROSE OR FIFTY US DOLLARS. Some jurisdictions do not allow certain limitations, so parts of this section may not apply to you.

14. Indemnification

You will defend and hold harmless the Keating maintainers and contributors from claims and costs (including reasonable legal fees) arising from your User Content, your use of the Service in violation of these Terms, or your violation of law or third-party rights.

15. Termination

You may stop using the Service at any time; local data remains under your control. We may suspend or terminate access to hosted features at any time for conduct that violates these Terms or threatens the Service. Sections that by their nature should survive (including 5, 9, and 12–14) survive termination.

16. Changes to the Service and these Terms

The Service evolves: hosted pages, demos, downloads, experiments, and provider integrations may be updated, paused, or removed. We may revise these Terms; when we do, we will update the “Last updated” date above, and for material changes we will take reasonable steps to give notice (such as a notice on the site or in release notes). Continued use after changes take effect constitutes acceptance.

17. General

These Terms are the entire agreement between you and the Keating maintainers regarding the hosted Service. If any provision is found unenforceable, the remainder stays in effect. Failure to enforce a provision is not a waiver. You may not assign these Terms without consent; we may assign them in connection with a reorganization of the project.

18. Contact

For questions about these Terms, open an issue in the Keating repository or use the contact channel listed on the project site.

import { Nav } from "../components/Nav"; import { Footer } from "../components/Footer"; import { useSeo } from "../hooks/useSeo"; const LAST_UPDATED = "July 1, 2026"; export function Terms() { useSeo({ title: "Terms of Service | Keating", description: "Terms of Service for Keating, the open-source hyperteacher and learning workspace at keating.help.", canonical: "https://keating.help/terms", }); return (
cat TERMS_OF_SERVICE.txt

Terms of Service

Last updated: {LAST_UPDATED}

1. Using Keating

Keating is an open-source teaching tool and learning workspace. You may use the website, browser app, CLI, and desktop app for lawful learning, research, and educational work. You are responsible for the prompts, files, API keys, provider credentials, and outputs you use with Keating.

2. Accounts, keys, and providers

Keating can connect to third-party AI providers and local runtimes that you configure. Those providers may have their own terms, pricing, data-handling rules, and rate limits. Keep your API keys and credentials secure. Do not share keys through prompts, public sessions, screenshots, or exported artifacts.

3. Educational content, not professional advice

Keating is designed to support study and reasoning. It does not provide legal, medical, financial, or other professional advice. Verify important information with appropriate sources and qualified professionals before acting on it.

4. User content and shared sessions

You retain responsibility for the content you enter, upload, import, export, or share. If you create a share link or publish an artifact, make sure you have the right to share that material and that it does not include private information you did not mean to disclose.

5. Acceptable use

Do not use Keating to harm others, break the law, bypass security controls, infringe rights, distribute malware, or abuse third-party services. We may limit or disable hosted features if they are used in a way that threatens reliability, safety, or legal compliance.

6. Open-source software

Keating is distributed under the license terms in its public repository. Repository code, package releases, and documentation may include separate notices for open-source dependencies. If there is a conflict between this website notice and the repository license for source code, the repository license governs the source code.

7. Availability and changes

Keating is provided as-is and may change over time. Hosted pages, demos, downloads, experiments, and provider integrations may be updated, paused, or removed. We will try to keep important learning paths available, but we do not guarantee uninterrupted service.

8. Contact

For questions about these terms, open an issue in the Keating repository or use the contact channel listed on the project site.

input.content:
import { Nav } from "../components/Nav"; import { Footer } from "../components/Footer"; import { DownloadPdfButton } from "../components/DownloadPdfButton"; import { useSeo } from "../hooks/useSeo"; const LAST_UPDATED = "July 1, 2026"; export function Privacy() { useSeo({ title: "Privacy Policy | Keating", description: "Privacy Policy for Keating, including local-first storage, provider keys, analytics, shared sessions, and P2P sync.", canonical: "https://keating.help/privacy", }); return (
cat PRIVACY_POLICY.txt

Privacy Policy

Last updated: {LAST_UPDATED}

1. The short version

Keating is local-first. Your conversations, study artifacts, settings, and provider keys are stored in your browser or on your device — not on a Keating account server, because there are no accounts. Data leaves your device only when a feature you invoke requires it: sending prompts to an AI provider you configured, publishing a share link, syncing between your own devices over P2P, or the product analytics described below.

2. Data stored on your device

The app keeps its working data in browser storage (localStorage and IndexedDB) or, in the desktop app, in its local data directory. This includes:

  • chat sessions, their titles, previews, and fork history;
  • study artifacts: plans, maps, quizzes, quiz answers and grades, goals, animations;
  • settings: interface preferences, teacher persona text, speech configuration, model visibility, proxy configuration;
  • provider API keys you enter;
  • cached copies of shared sessions you have opened.

We cannot see this data. It is also not backed up by us: clearing site data, uninstalling the app, or losing the device can erase it. Export or back up anything you cannot afford to lose.

3. Provider keys and AI requests

When you chat, the prompts, attached files, relevant session context, and your persona/system configuration are sent directly to the AI provider you selected (for example Anthropic, OpenAI, or Google), using your API key, under that provider’s privacy policy and data-retention rules. Keating does not proxy these requests through our servers unless you explicitly enable a proxy — and if you configure your own proxy URL, traffic goes to the proxy you chose.

Your keys stay in local settings storage. Do not paste keys or other secrets into chat messages, shared sessions, or exported artifacts; content in those places is handled as ordinary content, not as credentials.

4. Speech features

If you enable speech, audio for text-to-speech or transcription is sent to the speech provider you configured (or a custom endpoint you supply). Microphone access is requested from the browser only when you use voice input, and the browser controls that permission.

5. Shared sessions and public links

Creating a share link publishes the selected conversation or artifact. Depending on the share mode in your settings, content is encoded into the link itself and/or stored by the sharing backend so the link can resolve. Treat share links as public: anyone with the link can read the content, links can be forwarded, and copies may persist in caches even after deletion. Do not share content containing personal data you are not permitted to disclose.

6. Peer-to-peer sync (desktop)

The desktop app can replicate your data between your own devices using peer-to-peer components (encrypted topics and local writer identities). Data replicates to peers that hold your sync credentials; guard those like passwords. P2P sync is device-to-device and does not deposit your learning data on Keating servers.

7. Analytics and diagnostics

Hosted web pages use product analytics (currently PostHog) to understand feature usage, reliability, and release health — for example page views and events like “a session was shared”. We aim to collect product-level signals, not the content of your learning material: analytics events do not include your prompts, session content, or keys. Browser privacy settings, content blockers, and Do-Not-Track/consent tooling can limit or block this collection, and the app continues to work with analytics blocked.

8. Hosted infrastructure logs

Like any website, the hosted pages and share-link backend produce short-lived operational logs (IP address, user agent, requested URL, timestamps) used for security, abuse prevention, and debugging. These are retained only as long as needed for those purposes.

9. Cookies and similar technologies

Keating does not use advertising cookies. Browser storage is used for the app data described above, and the analytics tooling may set its own identifiers on hosted pages. There is no cross-site tracking by us.

10. Third-party services

Keating links to and integrates with third parties — AI providers, model hosts, GitHub, speech services, payment or credit systems where offered. Each is governed by its own privacy policy. Review a provider’s terms before sending it sensitive material; your configuration decides which third parties receive your data.

11. Your choices and rights

  • Delete local data at any time via browser site-data controls or by removing the desktop app’s data directory.
  • Remove saved provider keys in Settings.
  • Avoid share links, or remove shared content (cached copies held by recipients may persist).
  • Use local models to keep prompts entirely on your machine.
  • Block analytics with standard browser tooling.

Because we do not hold your learning data, most access/deletion rights are exercised directly on your own device. For questions about data we do hold (shared content, operational logs), contact us via the channel below and we will respond consistent with applicable data-protection law (such as the GDPR or CCPA where they apply).

12. Children

The hosted Service is not directed at children under 13 (or the higher minimum age your jurisdiction requires), and we do not knowingly collect personal information from them. Classroom deployments should be supervised by an educator or guardian who manages provider access.

13. Changes to this policy

We may update this policy as the Service evolves. We will update the “Last updated” date above and, for material changes, take reasonable steps to give notice on the site or in release notes.

14. Contact

For privacy questions about the repository or hosted site, open an issue in the Keating repository or use the contact channel listed on the project site.

cat PRIVACY_POLICY.txt

Privacy Policy

Last updated: {LAST_UPDATED}

1. The short version

Keating is local-first. Your conversations, study artifacts, settings, and provider keys are stored in your browser or on your device — not on a Keating account server, because there are no accounts. Data leaves your device only when a feature you invoke requires it: sending prompts to an AI provider you configured, publishing a share link, syncing between your own devices over P2P, or the product analytics described below.

2. Data stored on your device

The app keeps its working data in browser storage (localStorage and IndexedDB) or, in the desktop app, in its local data directory. This includes:

  • chat sessions, their titles, previews, and fork history;
  • study artifacts: plans, maps, quizzes, quiz answers and grades, goals, animations;
  • settings: interface preferences, teacher persona text, speech configuration, model visibility, proxy configuration;
  • provider API keys you enter;
  • cached copies of shared sessions you have opened.

We cannot see this data. It is also not backed up by us: clearing site data, uninstalling the app, or losing the device can erase it. Export or back up anything you cannot afford to lose.

3. Provider keys and AI requests

When you chat, the prompts, attached files, relevant session context, and your persona/system configuration are sent directly to the AI provider you selected (for example Anthropic, OpenAI, or Google), using your API key, under that provider’s privacy policy and data-retention rules. Keating does not proxy these requests through our servers unless you explicitly enable a proxy — and if you configure your own proxy URL, traffic goes to the proxy you chose.

Your keys stay in local settings storage. Do not paste keys or other secrets into chat messages, shared sessions, or exported artifacts; content in those places is handled as ordinary content, not as credentials.

4. Speech features

If you enable speech, audio for text-to-speech or transcription is sent to the speech provider you configured (or a custom endpoint you supply). Microphone access is requested from the browser only when you use voice input, and the browser controls that permission.

5. Shared sessions and public links

Creating a share link publishes the selected conversation or artifact. Depending on the share mode in your settings, content is encoded into the link itself and/or stored by the sharing backend so the link can resolve. Treat share links as public: anyone with the link can read the content, links can be forwarded, and copies may persist in caches even after deletion. Do not share content containing personal data you are not permitted to disclose.

6. Peer-to-peer sync (desktop)

The desktop app can replicate your data between your own devices using peer-to-peer components (encrypted topics and local writer identities). Data replicates to peers that hold your sync credentials; guard those like passwords. P2P sync is device-to-device and does not deposit your learning data on Keating servers.

7. Analytics and diagnostics

Hosted web pages use product analytics (currently PostHog) to understand feature usage, reliability, and release health — for example page views and events like “a session was shared”. We aim to collect product-level signals, not the content of your learning material: analytics events do not include your prompts, session content, or keys. Browser privacy settings, content blockers, and Do-Not-Track/consent tooling can limit or block this collection, and the app continues to work with analytics blocked.

8. Hosted infrastructure logs

Like any website, the hosted pages and share-link backend produce short-lived operational logs (IP address, user agent, requested URL, timestamps) used for security, abuse prevention, and debugging. These are retained only as long as needed for those purposes.

9. Cookies and similar technologies

Keating does not use advertising cookies. Browser storage is used for the app data described above, and the analytics tooling may set its own identifiers on hosted pages. There is no cross-site tracking by us.

10. Third-party services

Keating links to and integrates with third parties — AI providers, model hosts, GitHub, speech services, payment or credit systems where offered. Each is governed by its own privacy policy. Review a provider’s terms before sending it sensitive material; your configuration decides which third parties receive your data.

11. Your choices and rights

  • Delete local data at any time via browser site-data controls or by removing the desktop app’s data directory.
  • Remove saved provider keys in Settings.
  • Avoid share links, or remove shared content (cached copies held by recipients may persist).
  • Use local models to keep prompts entirely on your machine.
  • Block analytics with standard browser tooling.

Because we do not hold your learning data, most access/deletion rights are exercised directly on your own device. For questions about data we do hold (shared content, operational logs), contact us via the channel below and we will respond consistent with applicable data-protection law (such as the GDPR or CCPA where they apply).

12. Children

The hosted Service is not directed at children under 13 (or the higher minimum age your jurisdiction requires), and we do not knowingly collect personal information from them. Classroom deployments should be supervised by an educator or guardian who manages provider access.

13. Changes to this policy

We may update this policy as the Service evolves. We will update the “Last updated” date above and, for material changes, take reasonable steps to give notice on the site or in release notes.

14. Contact

For privacy questions about the repository or hosted site, open an issue in the Keating repository or use the contact channel listed on the project site.

import { Nav } from "../components/Nav"; import { Footer } from "../components/Footer"; import { useSeo } from "../hooks/useSeo"; const LAST_UPDATED = "July 1, 2026"; export function Privacy() { useSeo({ title: "Privacy Policy | Keating", description: "Privacy Policy for Keating, including local-first storage, provider keys, analytics, and shared sessions.", canonical: "https://keating.help/privacy", }); return (
cat PRIVACY_POLICY.txt

Privacy Policy

Last updated: {LAST_UPDATED}

1. What Keating stores locally

Keating is designed around local-first learning data. Browser sessions, settings, provider preferences, and study artifacts may be stored in your browser or desktop app storage so the workspace can work without a central account. Clearing browser or app data may remove that local history.

2. Provider keys and AI requests

When you configure external AI providers, your prompts, attachments, and generated context may be sent to those providers to produce responses. Provider keys should stay in your local settings or in server-side environment variables for hosted routes. Do not paste secrets into chat messages or shared sessions.

3. Shared sessions and public links

If you create or open a shared session link, the shared content may be encoded in the URL or stored through the sharing mechanism used by the app. Anyone with access to a public share link may be able to read the shared conversation or artifact. Review the content before sharing.

4. Analytics and diagnostics

Keating may use analytics or diagnostic tooling on hosted web pages to understand product usage, reliability, and release health. We aim to collect product-level signals rather than sensitive learning content. Browser privacy settings, blockers, and consent controls may limit this collection.

5. Desktop and P2P storage

The desktop app is intended to keep learning data on your device and, when enabled, replicate data through peer-to-peer components. P2P topics and local writer identities are technical synchronization details, not a substitute for careful handling of private study material.

6. Third-party services

Keating can link to GitHub, AI providers, payment or credit systems, model hosts, and other services. Those services are governed by their own privacy policies. Review their terms before sending sensitive material to them.

7. Your choices

You can delete local browser data, remove saved provider keys, avoid creating share links, use local models where available, or stop using hosted features. For repository or hosted-site privacy questions, open an issue or use the contact channel listed on the project site.

42 Keating is an open-source teaching tool and learning workspace. It is local-first: 43 most of your data lives in your own browser storage or on your own device, and the 44 Service connects to AI providers, runtimes, and sync mechanisms that you configure. 45 Parts of the Service are hosted (the website, shared-session publishing, downloads); 46 parts run entirely on your hardware. 47

1. The short version

28

29 Keating is local-first. Your conversations, study artifacts, settings, and provider 30 keys are stored in your browser or on your device — not on a Keating account server, 31 because there are no accounts. Data leaves your device only when a feature you invoke 32 requires it: sending prompts to an AI provider you configured, publishing a share 33 link, syncing between your own devices over P2P, or the product analytics described 34 below. 35

2. Data stored on your device

40

The app keeps its working data in browser storage (localStorage and IndexedDB) or, in the desktop app, in its local data directory. This includes:

41
    42
  • chat sessions, their titles, previews, and fork history;
    • 43
  • study artifacts: plans, maps, quizzes, quiz answers and grades, goals, animations;
    • 44
  • settings: interface preferences, teacher persona text, speech configuration, model visibility, proxy configuration;
    • 45
  • provider API keys you enter;
    • 46

49 We cannot see this data. It is also not backed up by us: clearing site data, 50 uninstalling the app, or losing the device can erase it. Export or back up anything 51 you cannot afford to lose. 52

57

Keating does not proxy these requests through 62 our servers unless you explicitly enable a proxy — and if you configure your own proxy 63 URL, traffic goes to the proxy you chose. 64

65

66 Your keys stay in local settings storage. Do not paste keys or other secrets into 67 chat messages, shared sessions, or exported artifacts; content in those places is 68 handled as ordinary content, not as credentials. 69

75 If you enable speech, audio for text-to-speech or transcription is sent to the speech 76 provider you configured (or a custom endpoint you supply). Microphone access is 77 requested from the browser only when you use voice input, and the browser controls 78 that permission. 79

content.text:
All work is complete and green. Here's the full picture:

## What was delivered

**Settings: 5 tabs → 3** — *Models & Providers* (keys, web search, visibility, custom models/providers + Proxy section), *Learning* (Teacher Persona + Speech & Voice with one merged section nav), *App* (interface settings, now with its own section nav). The mobile `<select>` was replaced with a chip tablist (keyboard-navigable, `dialog-compact-button` applied so the mobile full-width rule doesn't stretch them). New `useKeatingUiSettings` hook killed the stale-state bug where the Providers tab manually re-read localStorage — codex's review also caught and fixed a lost-update bug in the hook's merge.

**Sessions: 3 UIs → 1** — `SessionSidebar`, `SessionCardGrid`, and `SessionManagerDialog` are deleted, replaced by one adaptive `SessionBrowser` (single 1024px breakpoint): desktop keeps the resizable fork-tree panel with rename/delete/AI-suggest now inline per row (ported from the dialog, behind a `MoreHorizontal` menu); mobile gets the full-screen masonry card sheet using the whole viewport with safe-area padding. All list state (load, sort, search, tree, heroes, rename, remove, reload-on-change) lives in one shared `useSessions` hook. Codex's review fixed a real regression it found: non-AI-titled sessions couldn't be renamed from the mobile cards.

**Terms of Service & Privacy Policy** — both rewritten as comprehensive documents (18 and 14 sections: acceptable use, provider keys/billing responsibility, shared-link publicity, local-first data loss, PostHog analytics, P2P sync, warranties/liability, GDPR/CCPA posture, etc.), each with a **Download PDF** button. It uses the browser's print-to-PDF with a dedicated print stylesheet (nav/footer/chrome stripped, black-on-white, page-break-aware) — zero new dependencies. If you want a one-click `.pdf` file without the print dialog, that requires adding a PDF library; say the word.

## One thing you should know

`Terms.tsx` and `Privacy.tsx` were **untracked WIP files** in your working tree, and I replaced their content wholesale — since they were never committed, the previous versions aren't recoverable from git. The new pages follow the same retro-layout conventions and compile cleanly, but if the old ones had anything you specifically wanted kept, tell me and I'll re-add it.

## Verification

`bunx tsc --noEmit` clean, `bun test` 286 pass / 0 fail, production build succeeds. Worth a quick manual pass at 375px and 1440px: settings chips, session sheet open/close via hamburger, desktop row rename/suggest/delete, and printing `/terms` from the button.

Untouched as promised: `desktop/`, `packages/p2p-core/`, and your p2p WIP hunks in `Chat.tsx`/`app.css`/`keating-storage.ts`. Nothing is committed yet — the plan's stretch goals (merging the `googleGrounding`/`webSearch` toggles, decomposing the 973-line ProvidersModelsTab, `useMediaQuery` consolidation, settings deep links) are in the plan file whenever you want them.
Picking up the stretch goals from the plan. Quick check on the `googleGrounding`/`webSearch` semantics before I write the specs:

# Stretch batch A — settings storage cleanups (keating)

Repo: /home/diogenes/Projects/keating. Typecheck `cd web && bunx tsc --noEmit`; tests `cd web && bun test`. No new dependencies.
Do NOT touch `desktop/`, `packages/p2p-core/`, `web/src/pages/Chat.tsx`, `web/src/app.css`, `web/src/hooks/keating-storage.ts`.
SPECIAL FILE: `web/src/keating/nodepod-boot-files.ts` embeds a string snapshot of ui-settings.ts. Check whether a generator script produces it (search package.json scripts and scripts/ for "boot" or "nodepod"). If a generator exists, run it after the changes; if not, leave the embedded snapshot untouched (stale extra fields there are harmless) and report that.

## Task 1 — merge `googleGrounding` into `webSearch` (one-time migration)
`web/src/keating/ui-settings.ts`:
- Remove `googleGrounding` from `KeatingUiSettings` and `DEFAULT_UI_SETTINGS`. `webSearch: "auto" | "off"` becomes the single gate for Google grounding + OpenAI + Anthropic web search.
- Migration inside `normalizeSettings`: accept the legacy field on input (`value` may carry `googleGrounding`); result `webSearch` is `"off"` when `value.webSearch === "off"` OR (`value.webSearch` is undefined AND `value.googleGrounding === "off"`), else `"auto"`. Since normalizeSettings runs on load and save persists normalized output, the legacy key disappears on first save — no separate migration function needed. Type the transitional input as `Partial<KeatingUiSettings> & { googleGrounding?: "auto" | "off" }` or similar.
Consumers:
- `web/src/hooks/keating-stream.ts:34` — replace the `googleGrounding === "off"` gate with `webSearch === "off"`.
- `web/src/components/AssistantChatPanel.tsx:3150` — `groundingEnabled={uiSettings.webSearch === "auto"}` (surgical; this file is huge, change only this expression and any `googleGrounding` references you find in it).
- `web/src/components/KeatingUiSettingsTab.tsx` — remove the googleGrounding UI control if present (web search already has a control in the Providers tab); remove dead section-nav entry if the section disappears.
- Grep whole web/src for remaining `googleGrounding` (excluding nodepod-boot-files.ts unless regenerating) and clean up.
Tests: extend `web/src/test/ui-settings.test.ts`: legacy blob with `googleGrounding:"off"` and no `webSearch` loads as `webSearch:"off"`; legacy `googleGrounding:"off"` + explicit `webSearch:"auto"` stays `"auto"`; saved output contains no `googleGrounding` key.

## Task 2 — extract model prefs into `keating_model_prefs`
New module `web/src/keating/model-prefs.ts`:
- Owns `{ hiddenProviders: string[]; recentModels: Array<{key,timestamp}>; customModels: SavedModel[] }` under localStorage key `keating_model_prefs`, change event `keating:model-prefs-changed`, with `loadModelPrefs`, `saveModelPrefs` (normalize + dispatch), `subscribeModelPrefs` — mirror the ui-settings.ts patterns exactly (including the `storage` event listener for cross-tab).
- Move `addRecentModel`, `getRecentModels`, `addCustomModel`, `removeCustomModel`, `toggleProviderVisibility` here (same signatures). `SavedModel` type: move to model-prefs.ts and re-export from ui-settings.ts for compatibility.
- One-time migration inside `loadModelPrefs`: if `keating_model_prefs` is absent, read the raw `keating_ui_settings` blob and lift the three arrays (defaults if absent). Do NOT delete them from the old blob (harmless; ui-settings' normalizeSettings will drop them on next save once removed from its type).
- `ui-settings.ts`: remove the three fields from `KeatingUiSettings`, `DEFAULT_UI_SETTINGS`, and `normalizeSettings`; remove the moved helpers; keep a re-export of the moved helpers + SavedModel from ui-settings.ts so existing imports keep working (thin `export { ... } from "./model-prefs"`).
- New hook `web/src/hooks/use-model-prefs.ts`: `useModelPrefs(): [ModelPrefs, (patch: Partial<ModelPrefs>) => void]` mirroring `use-ui-settings.ts` (including the merge-against-latest-persisted fix that hook uses).
- Update consumers: grep web/src for `hiddenProviders|recentModels|customModels|getRecentModels|addRecentModel|addCustomModel|removeCustomModel|toggleProviderVisibility` and repoint reads from ui-settings state to model-prefs (e.g. ProvidersModelsTab uses `useModelPrefs()` alongside `useKeatingUiSettings()`; model-picker components using getRecentModels keep working via the re-exports but check any that read `settings.customModels` off the ui-settings object directly). Do not restructure components beyond the data source swap.
Tests: new `web/src/test/model-prefs.test.ts` — migration lifts legacy arrays from keating_ui_settings; helpers round-trip; subscriber notified on save. Update ui-settings tests that referenced the removed fields.

## Task 3 — shared local-setting helper (light persistence unification)
New `web/src/keating/local-setting.ts`:
```ts
export function createLocalSetting<T>(config: {
  key: string;            // localStorage key (unchanged from today)
  event: string;          // change event name (unchanged from today)
  normalize: (raw: unknown) => T;  // also supplies defaults
}): {
  load: () => T;
  save: (next: T) => void;             // persist normalized + dispatch event with detail
  subscribe: (cb: (value: T) => void) => () => void;  // event + cross-tab "storage"
}
```

# Stretch batch B — ProvidersModelsTab decomposition + settings deep links (keating)

Repo: /home/diogenes/Projects/keating. Typecheck `cd web && bunx tsc --noEmit`; tests `cd web && bun test`. No new dependencies.
Do NOT touch `desktop/`, `packages/p2p-core/`, `web/src/pages/Chat.tsx`, `web/src/app.css`, `web/src/hooks/keating-storage.ts`.
NOTE: batch A may have just landed model-prefs/webSearch changes in ProvidersModelsTab — read the current file, don't assume line numbers.

## Task 1 — decompose ProvidersModelsTab (~973 lines) into per-section files
Target layout under `web/src/components/settings/` (LearningTab.tsx and ModelsProvidersTab.tsx already live there):
- `CloudProviderKeysSection.tsx` — the "Cloud Providers" API-key management section
- `WebSearchSection.tsx` — the web search setting section
- `ProviderVisibilitySection.tsx` — visibility toggles
- `MyModelsSection.tsx` — saved/custom model list + add-model form
- `CustomProvidersSection.tsx` — custom provider CRUD
Rules:
- This is a MOVE refactor: relocate JSX + the state/handlers each section exclusively owns into its file. Shared state (e.g. the ui-settings/model-prefs hooks, provider key store access) stays in `ProvidersModelsTab.tsx`, passed down via props — keep prop surfaces small and explicit.
- Keep each section's `id="settings-section-*"` anchor and the top-level `SettingsSectionNav` (with `extraNavSections`) in ProvidersModelsTab.tsx, which becomes a thin composer (~100-150 lines).
- Zero behavior change. Match existing indentation style per file.
- Preserve the public export `ProvidersModelsTab({ extraNavSections })`.

## Task 2 — settings deep links (?settings=<tabId>)
- `web/src/hooks/useKeatingAgent.tsx`: on mount, read `new URLSearchParams(window.location.search).get("settings")`. If it matches a registered tab id (`models` | `learning` | `app`), open the settings dialog with that tab (the `SettingsDialog` `defaultTabId` prop already exists), then remove the param via `history.replaceState` (preserve other params + hash). Implement as a small `useEffect` near the existing settings dialog state; guard for SSR (`typeof window !== "undefined"`).
- Also accept legacy section ids gracefully: if the value is a known section anchor (e.g. `proxy`), map it to its tab (`proxy` → `models`) and, after the dialog opens, scroll to `settings-section-proxy` (reuse the scroll pattern from SettingsSectionNav; a setTimeout/rAF after open is fine). Keep this mapping minimal: `{ proxy: "models", persona: "learning", speech: "learning" }`.
- Make sure `defaultTabId` reaching SettingsDialog switches tabs when the dialog opens (it already has the effect; verify).

## Acceptance
- `bunx tsc --noEmit` clean, `bun test` green.
- `wc -l web/src/components/ProvidersModelsTab.tsx` ≤ ~200.
- Opening `/?settings=models`, `/?settings=app`, `/?settings=proxy` opens the dialog on the right tab (and scrolls for proxy); the URL is cleaned afterward. Describe how you verified (code trace is acceptable if no browser).
- Report files changed + deviations.

# Stretch batch C — useMediaQuery + session tree keyboard nav + ForkMapCard suggest (keating)

Repo: /home/diogenes/Projects/keating. Typecheck `cd web && bunx tsc --noEmit`; tests `cd web && bun test`. No new dependencies.
Do NOT touch `desktop/`, `packages/p2p-core/`, `web/src/pages/Chat.tsx` (it has its own matchMedia copy — leave it, WIP file), `web/src/app.css`, `web/src/hooks/keating-storage.ts`.

## Task 1 — shared useMediaQuery hook
- New `web/src/hooks/use-media-query.ts`: `export function useMediaQuery(query: string): boolean` — matchMedia with change listener, SSR-safe initial value (false when window undefined), stable across query changes.
- Adopt it in `web/src/components/SessionBrowser.tsx` (replace its local `useIsDesktop`, keep `SESSION_BROWSER_BREAKPOINT` exported and used in the query string).
- Grep web/src for other `window.matchMedia` call sites and adopt the hook where it's a mechanical swap in a React component (SKIP `web/src/pages/Chat.tsx` and any non-component module). List the call sites you converted and any you left.

## Task 2 — WAI-ARIA keyboard navigation for the desktop session tree
`web/src/components/SessionBrowserDesktop.tsx` (`role="tree"` list):
- Roving tabindex on the treeitem rows (the row's primary load button or the li itself — pick one focus target per row; tabIndex 0 on the active/first row, -1 elsewhere).
- Key handling on the tree container: ArrowDown/ArrowUp move focus through the flattened visible rows; ArrowRight expands a collapsed node (or moves to first child if already expanded); ArrowLeft collapses (or moves focus to parent if already collapsed/leaf); Home/End jump to first/last visible row; Enter/Space activates (loads) the focused session. Follow the WAI-ARIA tree pattern.
- Search mode (flat results) should still support ArrowUp/Down/Home/End/Enter (no expand/collapse).
- Don't fight existing mouse behavior or the row action menu; Escape inside the row menu keeps its current meaning.

## Task 3 — ForkMapCard AI-suggest
`web/src/components/ForkMapCard.tsx`: it gained `onRename`/`onDelete` recently (read the current file). Add optional `onSuggestTitle?: (id: string) => Promise<string>` prop threaded the same way `SessionCard.tsx` does (Sparkles button → await suggestion → enter rename mode prefilled). Wire it through from `SessionBrowserSheet.tsx` where ForkMapCard is rendered. Reuse SessionCard's pattern; keep tap targets ≥40px.

## Acceptance
- `bunx tsc --noEmit` clean, `bun test` green.
- Only one `useIsDesktop`-style matchMedia implementation remains under web/src outside Chat.tsx (the shared hook).
- Report files changed, matchMedia call sites converted/left, and deviations.
